coredata.dc:title: Coupling diazotization with oxidase-mimetic catalysis to realize dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengzhu
Zhao, Menghao
Liu, Peng
Zhu, Hengjia
Liu, Bangxiang
Hu, Panwang
Niu, Xiangheng
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite is often utilized as coloring and preservative agents in food industry, but excessive nitrite in drinking water and food causes great threats to human health, requiring convenient and reliable methods for its monitoring. In comparison with common single-signal assays, multimodal detection can not only expand application scenarios but also offer self-correction and self-validation to obtain more reliable results. However, how to achieve the multimodal sensing of nitrite remains a challenge. Here we report a novel dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical method for nitrite quantification by coupling diazotization with oxidase-mimicking catalysis. Carbon-supported Mn3O4 particles were explored as an oxidase-like nanozyme with excellent activity to catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue TMBox. On the one hand, the presence of nitrite can induce the diazotization of TMBox, not only decreasing the visible signal of TMBox at 652 nm but also generating a new signal assigned to diazotized TMBox at 445 nm. On the other hand, the existence of nitrite triggers the decrease of the TMB homogeneous electro-oxidation signal, but offers a new electro-oxidation signal attributed to nitrite itself. With such an interesting sensing principle, a dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical approach was developed to detect nitrite with good sensitivity and specificity. Reliable applications of the method in monitoring nitrite in several matrices were also demonstrated, implying its great promise in food safety supervision and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Dual-mode detection
Ratiometric colorimetry
Ratiometric electrochemistry
Diazotization
Nanozyme catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Diazotization of nitrite with TMBox produced from nanozyme catalysis
Dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite
Monitoring of nitrite in real samples
Nitrite induces the diazotization of TMBox produced from nanozyme catalysis
Dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The supports from the State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment (Grant no. ES202122 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21605061 ), the Cultivation Project for Excellent Young Teachers of Jiangsu University (Grant no. 4111310004 ) and the Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids of Ministry of Education (Grant no. FMS202001 ) are appreciated. M. Wang also appreciates the Scientific Research Project for College Students of Jiangsu University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) illustrates the preparation process of Mn3O4/C. (B) shows the XRD profile of Mn3O4/C. (C) depicts the TEM image of Mn3O4/C, and the inset is the size distribution histogram of Mn3O4/C particles. (D) depicts the HRTEM image of Mn3O4/C with lattice fringes. (E) displays elemental mapping images of Mn3O4/C. (F) and (G) present fine Mn 2p and O 1 s XPS profiles of Mn3O4/C, respectively.

(A) compares UV–vis spectra of different systems, and the inset shows the corresponding photograph (1-Mn3O4/C+TMB in air-saturated buffer; 2-Mn3O4/C+TMB in N2-saturated buffer; 3-Mn3O4/C; 4-TMB). (B) presents the EPR spectrum of the Mn3O4/C+DMPO system in ethanol. (C) records kinetic plots of Mn3O4/C catalyzing the chromogenic reaction of TMB at various concentrations, and (D) depicts the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk chart. (E) compares DPV curves of different systems recorded by SPE. (F) records the absorbance of the TMB oxidation reaction catalyzed by Mn3O4/C during a 40-day storage period.

(A) records the effect of nitrite on the UV–vis spectrum of the Mn3O4/C+TMB system, and the inset is the corresponding photograph (1-Mn3O4/C+TMB; 2-Mn3O4/C+TMB+NO2 -). (B) illustrates the diazotization reaction of TMBox and nitrite. (C) records the effect of nitrite on the electrochemical response of the Mn3O4/C+TMB system.

(A) displays the Mn3O4/C+TMB system with the existence of different levels of nitrite. (B) displays UV–vis spectra of the Mn3O4/C+TMB+NO2 - system with nitrite at different concentrations, and (C) depicts the relationship between the absorbance ratio A652/A445 and the logarithm of nitrite concentration. (D) displays DPV curves of the Mn3O4/C+TMB+NO2 - system with nitrite at different levels, and (E) depicts the relationship between the current ratio INitrite/ITMB and nitrite level. (F) and (G) record UV–vis and electrochemical responses of different species toward the Mn3O4/C+TMB+NO2 - system, respectively.
Schematic diagram for the dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite based on the cascade reaction of nanozyme catalysis and diazotization.

head.ce:title: Coupling diazotization with oxidase-mimetic catalysis to realize dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite is often utilized as coloring and preservative agents in food industry, but excessive nitrite in drinking water and food causes great threats to human health, requiring convenient and reliable methods for its monitoring. In comparison with common single-signal assays, multimodal detection can not only expand application scenarios but also offer self-correction and self-validation to obtain more reliable results. However, how to achieve the multimodal sensing of nitrite remains a challenge. Here we report a novel dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical method for nitrite quantification by coupling diazotization with oxidase-mimicking catalysis. Carbon-supported Mn3O4 particles were explored as an oxidase-like nanozyme with excellent activity to catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue TMBox. On the one hand, the presence of nitrite can induce the diazotization of TMBox, not only decreasing the visible signal of TMBox at 652 nm but also generating a new signal assigned to diazotized TMBox at 445 nm. On the other hand, the existence of nitrite triggers the decrease of the TMB homogeneous electro-oxidation signal, but offers a new electro-oxidation signal attributed to nitrite itself. With such an interesting sensing principle, a dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical approach was developed to detect nitrite with good sensitivity and specificity. Reliable applications of the method in monitoring nitrite in several matrices were also demonstrated, implying its great promise in food safety supervision and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 -), a common color-preserving, preservative and antimicrobial agent, is widely used in the food processing industry [1–3]. Too much nitrite intake, however, can cause various harms to human health [4,5]. For example, nitrite can bind with hemoglobin to form methemoglobin, causing the lack of oxygen fatal to infants [6]. More seriously, it reacts with amino acids to generate nitrosoamines, which are a class of strong carcinogens to induce gastric and esophageal cancers [7,8]. In this regard, the World Health Organization has set the limit of nitrite in drinking water as 3 mg/L (approximately 65 μM) [9], and the Joint Food and Agriculture Organization/World Health Organization Expert Committee on Food Additives has ruled the maximum intake of nitrite as 0.06 mg/kg/day [10]. Hence, it is very important to develop simple and fast methods to monitor nitrite in food and environmental matrices.

Up to now, a variety of analytical methods have been developed for the detection of nitrite in food and environmental samples, including chromatography [11–14], capillary electrophoresis [15–17], fluorescent probes [18–22], colorimetric approaches [23–25] and electrochemical sensors [26,27]. Compared to chromatography and capillary electrophoresis, the latter three methods show the advantages of convenience operation, low cost and rapid response, and have attracted special attention for nitrite quantification [28–30]. Nevertheless, almost all of these sensing methods are based on target-induced single-signal changes, which are susceptible to external conditions and possibly lead to incorrect results. More seriously, single-signal modes in some cases cannot realize the detection purpose. For example, a single-mode colorimetric method cannot detect colored samples. In contrast, multi-mode detection can be applied to different scenarios because the users are able to select a suitable sensing mode according to the test environment. Besides, the results provided by multimodal detection can be verified mutually, thus greatly improving the analytical reliability [31,32]. Accordingly, it is quite necessary to establish multi-mode detection methods with good reliability in practical applications for nitrite monitoring. However, how to establish a robust but simple system to achieve such a goal turns to be a challenge.

In recent years, nanomaterials with enzyme-like catalytic features (nanozymes) have attracted widespread interest due to their characteristics of low cost, good stability and strong adaptability to the environment [33–35]. Especially in the biochemical sensing field, nanozymes show the merit of providing catalytic amplification signals for high-sensitivity detection, and have been widely utilized in detecting various analytes [36–41]. In the present work, we couple the specific diazotization reaction with oxidase-mimetic catalysis together and develop a novel dual-mode double-ratiometric electrochemical and colorimetric method for nitrite sensing. As illustrated in Scheme 1, carbon-supported Mn3O4 particles (Mn3O4/C) are designed to act as an oxidase-mimicking nanozyme with excellent activity to effectively catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue TMBox. When the target nitrite is added, the intermediate TMBox produced from nanozyme catalysis can be diazotized by nitrite. The diazotization process, on the one hand, is able to reduce the visible signal of TMBox at 652 nm but provide a new signal assigned to diazotized TMBox at 445 nm, and on the other hand, leads to the decrease of the TMB homogeneous electro-oxidation signal but offers a new electro-oxidation signal attributed to nitrite itself. Therefore, such reversed changes of both the optical and electrical signals enable us to realize the dual-mode double-ratiometric sensing of nitrite with good performance. The inherent superiorities offered by multimodal analysis and ratiometric calculation also make it possible to monitor the analyte in actual matrices with excellent reliability and practicability.

MnSO4·H2O, L-methionine (L-Met), NaOH, ethanol, NaNO2, HAc, NaAc and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were gained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. TMB, ascorbic acid (AA) and glutamic acid (Glu) were gained from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd. 2,2′-Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) was procured from Sigma-Aldrich. All other chemicals and reagents used in this study had the analytical purity. Deionized water (DW) was used in the whole research.

The proposed Mn3O4/C was synthesized via a two-step procedure ( Fig. 1A). First, 5 mmol L-Met and 5 mmol NaOH were dissolved in 20 mL DW, and MnSO4 solution (2.5 mM, 10 mL) was slowly added to the above mixture and kept stirring for 15 min, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The gained solid was washed with ethanol and DW three times and dried in a vacuum oven. Then, the dried product was calcinated in a tube furnace at 500 ℃ in air for 2 h to obtain the material Mn3O4/C.

An XRD-6100Lab diffractometer (Shimadzu) enabled X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests. A Regulus-8100 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi) and a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI) were utilized to probe the shape of materials. A Nicolet Nexus 470 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet) was applied to probe the material. An A3000–10/12 electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR, Bruker) provided EPR spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were done by a K-Alpha+ spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher).

The oxidase-mimicking catalytic characteristics of Mn3O4/C were researched by exploiting TMB as a main substrate with the existence of dissolved O2. In detail, 25 μL of 0.25 mg/mL Mn3O4/C suspension and 30 μL of 5 mM TMB solution were added to 945 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.5, 0.2 M). After reaction for 5 min, the absorbance of the mixture was recorded by an Evolution 201 UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Thermo Scientific). Besides, the reaction of Mn3O4/C and TMB with the presence of dissolved O2 was also studied by electrochemical measurements carried out on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.). The workstation was equipped with an integrated screen-printed electrode (SPE) composed of a carbon-based working electrode (its active surface area was approximately 0.071 cm2), an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a carbon counter electrode. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique was employed to record the electrochemical signal from −0.1–0.7 V versus the reference electrode, where increased potential was 4 mV, amplitude was 0.05 V, pulse width was 0.06 s, sample width was 0.02 s, and pulse period was 0.5 s

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diazotization of nitrite with TMBox produced from nanozyme catalysis
ce:section.ce:para: To study the interaction between nitrite and the TMBox intermediate produced, 25 μL Mn3O4/C suspension (0.25 mg/mL) and 30 μL TMB solution (5 mM) were mixed in 925 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.5, 0.2 M). After incubation at room temperature for 5 min, 20 μL nitrite solution (5 mM) was added to the above mixture for reaction for another 10 min. After that, the solution absorbance was recorded by UV–vis. Also, the electrochemical response of the system was recorded by DPV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed dual-mode double-ratiometric detection method was verified by testing nitrite at different concentrations. Under optimal conditions, 25 μL Mn3O4/C suspension (0.25 mg/mL) and 30 μL TMB solution (5 mM) were added to NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.5, 0.2 M). After reaction for 5 min, different concentrations of nitrite were added to the above mixture and incubated at room temperature for another 10 min. Finally, the UV–vis and DPV responses were recorded according to the above protocol accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of nitrite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of the developed method, it was utilized to analyze nitrite in practical samples, including two environmental water samples collected from Yangtze River (Zhenjiang, China) and Yudai River (Jiangsu University, China), homemade pickle water, and salted eggs obtained from a local market (Zhenjiang, China). The three water samples were centrifuged to remove solid impurities and measured directly. For salted eggs, 5 g sample was weighed and added to 12.5 mL saturated borax solution (50 mg/mL). After bathing in boiling water for 15 min, the sample was cooled to room temperature, and 5 mL K4[Fe(CN)6] solution (106 mg/mL) was added and shook well. After that, 5 mL Zn(Ac)2 solution (220 mg/mL) was added and stirred for 30 min. Finally, the supernatant was extracted by high-speed centrifugation for analysis. All the samples were monitored based on the protocol mentioned above. At the same time, a commercial nitrite kit gained from Beijing Solarbio Science and Technology Co., Ltd. was employed as a reference method to monitor these samples accordingly.

The material Mn3O4/C was prepared by calcinating a Mn-based amino acid complex in air (Fig. 1A). First, a mixing process was applied to synthesize the Mn-based amino acid complex precursor, and then the collected solid precursor was calcinated at 500 ℃ for 2 h, thus gaining the proposed material. Such a procedure is very simple, and all the raw materials are available commercially at low cost, making it possible to produce the material on a large scale for potential applications.

To verify the preparation of Mn3O4/C, the synthetic process was investigated by several characterization means. Fig. 1B presents the XRD profile of the collected material. It exhibits some characteristic peaks at 18.01°, 28.91°, 32.38°, 36.08°, 38.09°, 44.41°, 50.84° and 59.91°, which are well in agreement with the (101), (112), (103), (211), (004), (220), (105) and (224) crystal planes of Mn3O4 (PDF number: 80–0382), respectively [42], indicative of the Mn3O4 structure of the prepared material. In addition, two obvious carbon signals (marked with asterisks) are also observed in the XRD profile, implying that the Mn3O4 structure is supported on a carbon carrier. As shown by the TEM image (Fig. 1C), the achieved Mn3O4/C shows an irregular particle shape, with the size varying from 20 nm to 70 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image exhibits notable lattice fringes with a space of 0.308 nm (Fig. 1D), which should be ascribed to the (112) lattice plane of Mn3O4 [43]. Both the EDS (Fig. S1, SI) and elemental mapping results (Fig. 1E) confirm the existence of Mn, O and C in the obtained material. Besides, a small amount of N and S are also probed, which should originate from the L-Met precursor.

The FTIR spectrum of Mn3O4/C (Fig. S2, SI) shows a few characteristic peaks at 3424, 1142, 625 and 514 cm−1. The 3424 cm−1 peak corresponds to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl remained in the carbon carrier, and the 1142 cm−1 one is attributed to the O-H bending vibration [44]. The FTIR peak at 625 cm−1 should be attributed to the Mn-O stretching vibration at tetrahedral site, while the one at 514 cm−1 is assigned to the Mn-O vibration at octahedral position [45–47]. The XPS spectrum shows that Mn, O, C and S coexist in the obtained Mn3O4/C (Fig. S3, SI), in consistent with the EDS and elemental mapping results. In the high-resolution Mn 2p XPS (Fig. 1F), the signals of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 are located at 641.5 and 653.1 eV, respectively, which can further be deconvoluted to Mn2+ (641.0 and 652.1 eV), Mn3+ (642.3 and 653.3 eV) and Mn4+ (645.8 and 655.1 eV) with corresponding shake-up peaks [48,49]. As depicted in Fig. 1G, the binding energies at approximately 529.4, 531.1 and 532.0 eV are assigned to lattice oxygen Mn-O bond (OL), surface oxygen vacancy (OV) and surface adsorbed oxygen (Oads), respectively [42,48]. The C 1 s deconvoluted spectrum yields three peaks centered at 284.2, 285.3 and 287.9 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-O and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S4, SI) [50,51]. All the proofs well confirm the successful preparation of the proposed material Mn3O4/C.

To evaluate the oxidase-mimicking characteristics of Mn3O4/C, a typical chromogenic reaction was carried out by employing TMB as a common substrate. As depicted in Fig. 2A, the colorless substrate is oxidized to a blue product in the presence of Mn3O4/C, and an obvious absorbance change can be observed at 652 nm. Conversely, neither apparent color nor UV–vis absorbance changes are observed in the absence of Mn3O4/C or TMB. Besides, in N2-bubbled solution the TMB oxidation reaction is suppressed significantly, confirming the pivotal role of dissolved O2 in the Mn3O4/C+TMB catalytic system [48,52,53]. This phenomenon confirms that Mn3O4/C indeed plays an oxidase-like role in the catalytic oxidation of TMB. The study on the oxidation mechanism of TMB suggests that the TMB substrate can be oxidized to form a radical cation via a one-electron process (Fig. S5, SI), which further forms a semiquinone-imine in equilibrium with a charge-transfer complex of diamine (electron donor) and diimine (electron acceptor) [54], thus exhibiting a blue color. Here the diamine-diimine charge-transfer complex formed from TMB oxidization is noted as TMBox. Besides, the obtained Mn3O4/C can catalyze the oxidation of OPD and ABTS as well (Fig. S6, SI) to generate corresponding products with respective color changes. To further verify the Mn3O4/C catalytic mechanism, EPR spectra with the absence or presence of Mn3O4/C were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2B, compared to the blank group (only the capture agent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-1-oxide (DMPO) exists), the Mn3O4/C+DMPO group obviously captures the signals assigned to superoxide radicals. The EPR proof proves that the material Mn3O4/C plays an oxidase-mimetic role in catalyzing the TMB color reaction via a superoxide radical pathway. In detail, the nanozyme Mn3O4/C first catalyzes the conversion of dissolved O2 to form superoxide radicals, and then the latter with strong oxidability oxidizes the substrate to its corresponding product.

Generally, the catalytic activity of a nanozyme highly depends on its reaction conditions. In the present study, effects of reaction time, nanozyme dosage, buffer pH and temperature on oxidase-like Mn3O4/C catalyzing the TMB color reaction were explored. As shown in Fig. S7A (SI), with the increase of reaction time the absorbance at 652 nm gradually increases until reaches to a balance and stable state after 5 min. Therefore, the TMB oxidation time is set as 5 min thereafter. As depicted in Fig. S7B (SI), the apparent catalytic activity is significantly improved with the increase of nanozyme dosage (2.5–10.0 μg/mL), and a medium dosage (6.25 μg/mL) is selected for the following research. When the buffer pH ranges from 3.0 to 8.0, the nanozyme Mn3O4/C exhibits different enzyme-mimetic activities, with the maximum catalytic activity obtained at pH 3.5 (Fig. S7C, SI). The proposed Mn3O4/C can show good catalytic activity over a broad temperature ranging from 25 °C to 65 °C (Fig. S7D, SI). With the consideration of operation convenience, room temperature (25 °C) is chosen in subsequent experiments.

Under the above conditions, steady-state kinetic analysis was performed to further evaluate the oxidase-mimicking ability of Mn3O4/C. As displayed in Fig. 2C, the initial reaction rate increases with the increase of TMB concentration and gradually tends to be stable. The steady-state kinetic parameters of oxidase-mimicking Mn3O4/C can be derived from the Michaelis-Menten mode. As shown in the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal graph (Fig. 2D), the apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) is determined to be 0.06 mM, and the maximum velocity (V max) is 1.04 × 10−7 M/s. As compared in Table S1 (SI), the K m value of Mn3O4/C is less than other oxidase-like nanozymes, indicating its stronger affinity toward the substrate.

Also, electrochemical measurements were employed to study the oxidase-like catalytic feature of Mn3O4/C toward TMB. The DPV technique was used to measure the oxidation signals of free TMB, Mn3O4/C and Mn3O4/C+TMB. As indicated in Fig. 2E, it is found that the TMB substrate can produce two independent anodic peaks. This is because the electro-oxidation of TMB takes place through two successive single-electron processes [55]. In aqueous solution TMB is first electro-oxidized to semiquinone-imine, which is further oxidized to diimine (Fig. S8, SI). When Mn3O4/C and TMB coexist, the nanozyme Mn3O4/C rapidly catalyzes TMB to TMBox. Due to the consumption of the TMB substrate, the two oxidation peaks of TMB decrease accordingly, in agreement with the result reported in the literature [56]. The electrochemical results once again prove the oxidase-like catalytic activity of Mn3O4/C.

Given stability and reproducibility are crucial for a nanozyme catalyst in analytical applications, we assessed the storage stability of Mn3O4/C catalyzing the oxidation of TMB. As found in Fig. 2F, the catalytic activity of Mn3O4/C exhibits no apparent change in a 40-day storage period (Fig. 2F), indicating excellent stability of the proposed Mn3O4/C as an artificial enzyme. In addition, we studied the batch reproducibility of Mn3O4/C, and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.5% is gained for the material obtained from five different batches (Table S2, SI), demonstrating good reproducibility of the nanozyme prepared. With such excellent stability and reproducibility, the proposed nanozyme Mn3O4/C is supposed to find wide applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrite induces the diazotization of TMBox produced from nanozyme catalysis
ce:section.ce:para: Under the catalysis of Mn3O4/C, the Mn3O4/C+TMB system presents a blue color due to the oxidation of TMB to TMBox, while the presence of nitrite can further lead to the color change of the Mn3O4/C+TMB system. In detail, the Mn3O4/C+TMB solution gradually becomes yellow-green from blue when a small concentration of nitrite is added (the inset in Fig. 3A). Correspondingly, the UV–vis absorption signal centered at 652 nm is weakened, while a new peak located at 445 nm is observed when nitrite exists (Fig. 3A). Such a phenomenon observed in Fig. 3A should be closely related to the diazotization reaction between the TMBox intermediate and nitrite. To demonstrate this conclusion, the AA reduction test was carried out. As demonstrated in Fig. S9 (SI), the nanozyme Mn3O4/C can catalyze TMB at low (0.006 mM), medium (0.03 mM) and high (0.15 mM) concentrations to generate yellow overoxidized TMBox, green TMBox mixed with overoxidized TMBox, and blue TMBox, respectively. When 100 μM AA is added, they are all reduced to colorless TMB again [53,57]. Nonetheless, when nitrite exists, TMBox and nitrite undergo the diazotization reaction to form diazotized TMBox. Given AA can reduce only the TMBox intermediate but not diazotized TMBox, the result suggests that the typical diazotization reaction indeed takes place between TMBox and nitrite. As explained in Fig. 3B, in an acidic environment (pH 3.5) nitrite can react with the aromatic primary amine group of the TMBox intermediate to form a diazonium salt (diazotizated TMBox) [58], thus resulting in the signal weakening at 652 nm (TMBox) and enhancement at 445 nm (diazotizated TMBox).

Also, when nitrite is added to the Mn3O4/C+TMB system, the anodic peaks of TMB decrease again, and a new anodic signal appears at around 0.47 V (Fig. 3C). This is because the TMBox intermediate diazotizes with nitrite in the acidic environment, which further consumes the TMB species existing in the diamine-diimine charge-transfer complex form, thus resulting in the signal decline of TMB electro-oxidation. Meanwhile, in the acidic solution nitrite can be electrochemically oxidized to nitrate (Fig. S10, SI), providing the new anodic signal observed at 0.47 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: Given nitrite can trigger both the color and electrical reversed changes of the Mn3O4/C+TMB system (Fig. 3A and C), we can use these signal changes to establish a dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical platform for nitrite detection. To better monitor the target, the diazotization time between nitrite and TMBox was studied. As shown in Fig. S11 (SI), the ratio of the absorbance values at 652 nm and 445 nm (A652/A445) decreases along with the increase of diazotization time, and after 10 min a saturation effect gradually appears. Therefore, 10 min is determined as the optimized diazotization time for detecting nitrite.

Under optimal conditions, the content of nitrite was sensed by ratiometric colorimetry first. As shown in Fig. 4A, when the concentration of nitrite gradually increases, the color of the Mn3O4/C+TMB+NO2 - solution changes from blue to green and further to yellow. Accordingly, the absorbance at 652 nm decreases and the one at 445 nm increases (Fig. 4B). It is further found that the absorbance ratio A652/A445 linearly decreases against the increase of the logarithm of nitrite content in the scope of 3–150 μM (Fig. 4 C). The fitting equation can be described as Y = 5.283 – 2.252X (μM, R2 = 0.988). According to the three-fold signal-to-noise (S/N = 3) rule, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.4 μM. It should be noted that, given nitrite can directly react with the amino group of TMB in the acidic environment to form diazonium salts with a notable signal at 445 nm [58,59], it can also be utilized to detect the analyte directly. The interactions between nitrite and TMB involve two competitive processes, namely the diazotization reaction between nitrite and TMB as well as their redox reaction. Since both the signals at 445 nm and 652 nm require the consumption of nitrite simultaneously, a ratiometric detection mode cannot be achieved based on the principle. As depicted in Fig. S12A (SI), the UV–vis peak at 445 nm increases along with the content of nitrite, while the one at 652 nm changes with no particular pattern. By taking the 445 nm peak as the indicator, a linear relationship is gained in the content scope of 10–300 μM (Fig. S12B, SI), with an LOD of 0.9 μM. As verified, the proposed ratiometric colorimetric mode realized by the coupling of diazotization and oxidase-mimicking catalysis performs better compared to the single-signal one in terms of detection limit and sensitivity. Also, the content of nitrite was determined by ratiometric electrochemistry under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4D, when the level of nitrite increases, the anodic signal attributed to TMB electro-oxidation gradually decreases, while the one assigned to the electro-oxidation of nitrite itself increases. In the range of 3–250 μM the concentration of nitrite and the electrochemical ratio INitrite/ITMB have a good linear relationship (Fig. 4E), and the regression equation is noted as Y = 0.0252 + 0.0034X (μM, R2 = 0.995), with an LOD of 1.7 μM. In comparison with previously reported nitrite detection methods (Table S3, SI), the results show that the proposed dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical method offers comparable detection range and LOD, indicating its great promise in real applications.

In addition to sensitivity, selectivity is also an important parameter affecting the accurate analysis of nitrite. In this study we evaluated the selectivity of the double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical method for the detection of nitrite (100 μM) with the presence of other species (200 μM). As can be seen in Fig. 4F and G, 16 kinds of potential interferences (Na+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, As3+, As5+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cl-, CO3 2-, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, urea, Glu and glucose) are used to study the detection selectivity of the Mn3O4/C+TMB system toward nitrite, and only the target nitrite leads to a significant absorbance ratio decrease of the Mn3O4/C+TMB+NO2 - system. The impacts of other coexisting ions on the absorbance or electrochemical ratio can be negligible, indicating that the dual-mode double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical method established has good selectivity for the detection of nitrite. Besides, the established method is very effective in repeatedly detecting nitrite at different concentrations (Fig. S13, SI).

Finally, to check the analytical feasibility of the dual-mode double-ratiometric nitrite detection strategy in the fields of environmental detection and food safety, real samples collected from difference matrices were tested. The concentrations of nitrite in real samples, including Yangtze River water, Yudai River water, pickle water and salted eggs, were determined by a standard addition method. As summarized in Table S4 (SI), the ratiometric colorimetric and ratiometric electrochemical method offers good recoveries, indicating that the established platform has potential applicability to the analysis of nitrite in complex samples. Furthermore, a commercial detection kit based on a Griess reagent was utilized to test these samples and compared with our method. The obtained results also show that our method can provide good reliability and feasibility.

In comparison with common single-signal/single-mode nitrite assays, the ratiometric colorimetric mode provided by our dual-mode method can directly monitor nitrite at the naked eye level, and the electrochemical mode can effectively overcome the possible color interference from real samples. Therefore, our dual-mode method can be applied to different scenarios according to the sample conditions. More importantly, the results provided by the dual-mode double-ratiometric method can be verified mutually and self-corrected, which can greatly improve the detection accuracy and reliability. With these attractive features as well as simple operation, the proposed multimodal approach will find promising applications in monitoring nitrite in food and environmental matrices.

In summary, we have validated a new multimodal method for the double-ratiometric colorimetric and electrochemical detection of nitrite via coupling nanozyme catalysis with diazotization together. By exploring carbon-supported Mn3O4 particles as a high-efficiency oxidase mimic to catalyze TMB oxidation to TMBox, followed by the target-induced specific diazotization reaction, both visible color and electrical signals with the ratiometric paradigm have been gained to sense nitrite with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Practical use of the developed method in analyzing the target in environmental water and food matrices has also been verified. Compared to traditional single-mode/single-signal sensors, our dual-mode double-ratiometric approach shows attractive merits in terms of expanding application scenarios and improving detection reliability. With these merits as well as convenient operation, the proposed method is believed to find promising applications in the areas of food safety and environmental monitoring.

Mengzhu Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology. Menghao Zhao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Methodology. Peng Liu: Validation, Software, Methodology. Hengjia Zhu: Validation, Software, Methodology. Bangxiang Liu: Validation, Software. Panwang Hu: Validation, Software. Xiangheng Niu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The supports from the State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment (Grant no. ES202122), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21605061), the Cultivation Project for Excellent Young Teachers of Jiangsu University (Grant no. 4111310004) and the Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids of Ministry of Education (Grant no. FMS202001) are appreciated. M. Wang also appreciates the Scientific Research Project for College Students of Jiangsu University.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mengzhu Wang is a master candidate under the supervision of Dr. Niu, and she is interested in nitrite detection based on nanozyme catalysis.
Menghao Zhao is an undergraduate of Jiangsu University, and he focuses on the fabrication of sensing systems using novel nanozymes.
Peng Liu is a master candidate in Dr. Niu’s group, and he is interested in nanozyme-based pesticide detection.
Hengjia Zhu is a doctoral candidate under the supervision of Dr. Niu, and he focuses on nanozyme-based sensors for pesticide residue detection.
Bangxiang Liu is a master candidate, and he is interested in nanozyme-based analysis.
Panwang Hu is a master candidate in Jiangsu University, and he is interested in molecularly imprinted nanozymes.
Xiangheng Niu is an Associate Professor in Jiangsu University, and he focuses on developing analytical methods based on efficient nanozymes.
